data.title: Uber extends rally for a seventh straight session
data.description:
Shares of Uber Technologies (UBER) rose 0.6% to close at $76.82 on Wednesday, adding to a six-session winning streak.

The stock has gained around 7% between February 24 and March 3 compared to a 1.07% fall of the S&P 500 during the same period.

The move comes amid recent developments, including coverage of urban policy shifts after a judge ruled the Trump administration’s move to end New York City’s congestion toll was unlawful, continued attention on autonomous driving after Wayve raised $1.2B from backers including Microsoft, Nvidia, and Uber, and updates on Uber’s push into aerial mobility alongside Joby. The company also announced plans to acquire parking app SpotHero, adding to strategic expansion efforts during the period.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system, UBER is rated Hold, with a score of 3.12 out of 5, receiving an A+ for profitability, but offset by a D- in terms of momentum.

A Seeking Alpha analysis said Uber has become a volatile “battleground” stock as investors debate whether autonomous vehicles represent a threat or tailwind, adding that “the unit economics of AV technology could offer a tailwind for Uber Technologies.”

The analyst noted that “70% of contra-revenue… expenses are driver-related,” arguing that eliminating human-driver costs could lift profitability, and pointed out that Uber’s “200 million+ user network” gives it leverage with AV partners. The note also highlighted diversification, stating that “approximately 50% of total revenue is generated internationally” and “approximately 50% of total gross bookings is generated through the delivery segment.”

On Wall Street, 47 of 56 analysts rate the stock Buy or higher, eight recommend Hold, and one recommends a Strong Sell.

Shares have gained around 1.85% in the past one month, and is down approximately 7.15% year-to-date.

data.title: Netflix shares uptick for seven consecutive sessions; add over 25% in last six sessions
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) shares clocked seven straight sessions of gains, as the stock rose 1.1% at $98.75 on Wednesday.

The movies and entertainment giant gained 25.2% in the preceding six sessions. The stock has added over 5% in the year.

NFLX is up 19% over the past one month.

Netflix abruptly withdrew from its bid for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) in late February, many in Hollywood were caught off guard. 

Most recently, Netflix announced that it is expanding its Ads Suite to give brands more options to buy and measure ads, with a focus on better targeting, frequency management, and scaled reach on its ad-supported plan. 

"Management’s disciplined approach avoids integration risk, preserves balance sheet strength, and positions Netflix for gradual, compelling standalone improvements," pointed out a recent Seeking Alpha analysis.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, NFLX has a Buy rating with a score of 3.1 out of 5. The company received A+ in the prospect of profitability, while it received D- in valuation.

Turning to the Wall Street community, 36 analysts gave NFLX a Buy and above rating. 11 analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, while one recommended Sell.

Seeking Alpha analysts are positive and see the stock as a Buy.

data.title: Rayonier Advanced Materials targets positive free cash flow and substantial EBITDA growth in 2026 through pricing and portfolio initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rayonier Advanced Materials (RYAM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Scott Sutton opened by identifying negative free cash flow of $88 million and high-cost debt as unsustainable, declaring "priority 1...is simple: deliver positive free cash flow in 2026" and stating the company's aim "to exit 2026 with significant momentum with a heavy focus on execution." Sutton emphasized progress in Cellulose Specialties, reporting that "85% of the Specialties business is now arranged at an average price increase of 18% over 2025 with expected volume loss of about 20% compared with 2025," while negotiations on the remaining 15% are ongoing and expected to yield even higher price increases. Sutton also underscored a broad, company-wide playbook to improve EBITDA in 2026 relative to 2025, targeting improvement in every business segment through leadership initiatives, active portfolio management, and new product commercialization.
  • CFO Marcus Moeltner stated, "improved performance based on execution on those themes will position RYAM for refi to really address the capital structure and drive down our interest expense and fixed charges."

Outlook

  • Sutton outlined the plan to deliver positive free cash flow and achieve "a full year EBITDA substantially better than 2025, along with solid positive free cash flow." The company expects to bridge a near zero EBITDA first quarter as new leadership initiatives take effect, with every business expected to improve its EBITDA performance over 2025.
  • Sutton described ongoing negotiations for the remaining 15% of Specialties business, indicating that any successful agreements will come at a price increase significantly higher than the 18% already achieved.
  • The CEO described a "NASCAR leaderboard strategy" for managing product mix dynamically, optimizing asset utilization, and maximizing contribution profit by shifting production across market segments as conditions warrant.

Financial Results

  • Sutton reported that free cash flow for 2025 was negative $88 million and stressed the need to reverse this in the coming year.
  • Management described achievements in pricing, particularly the near 20% price increase for ether-grade cellulose in Europe despite demand challenges, and more than 18% price increases in nitration-grade cellulose.
  • There were no explicit EPS or revenue figures stated for the quarter in the transcript.

Q&A

  • Daniel Harriman, Sidoti, asked about internal confidence in earnings power and long-term value, and whether higher pricing in Cellulose Specialties could attract competitors. Sutton responded that "our best opportunity really comes from the team" and highlighted the company's unique position as the last two domestic producers, noting, "we've got a long way to go before we get to reinvestment economics."
  • Matthew McKellar, RBC, inquired about rejecting a potential offer and the rationale for remaining independent. Sutton declined to comment on specific offers but said, "we have plans that will deliver substantially more value" and outlined four core value creation themes centering on leadership initiatives, dynamic asset allocation, new product development, and inflation offset strategies. Moeltner added that improved execution would support refinancing efforts.
  • McKellar asked about demand and competition dynamics. Sutton described ether-grade cellulose as "challenged a bit" in Europe due to Chinese imports but said RYAM still achieved near 20% price increases. For nitration-grade, there is "lots of new inquiries" and demand, especially for domestic propellant producers.
  • Dmitry Silversteyn, Water Tower Research, asked about strategy for Biomaterials following the withdrawal from the Georgia energy project and operational issues at Tartas. Sutton stressed an integrated approach across all businesses and cited plans to run Tartas harder to increase feedstock for Biomaterials, particularly for the BioNova JV. Silversteyn also queried the timeline for trade remedy cases; Sutton expects a preliminary countervailing duties determination in March and antidumping duties in May.
  • Harriman asked about the longer-term portfolio fit for Paperboard and High-Yield Pulp. Sutton stated, "we're not selling any business, and we're not closing any assets" and expects improvement in both segments through new product commercialization and recovery from oversupply.
  • McKellar followed up on the integrated model strategy and Paperboard opportunities following a competitor's mill closure. Sutton and Moeltner explained the flexible, margin-maximizing approach and noted improved volumes and quality in Paperboard, while also highlighting the need to absorb new U.S. capacity.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed persistent concerns about the sustainability of recent price increases, competitive dynamics, and execution of strategic initiatives. Questions were pointed but constructive, with a focus on unlocking value and mitigating external risks.
  • Management tone was determined and pragmatic in prepared remarks, but at times defensive and measured during Q&A, particularly regarding past performance, pricing ceilings, and portfolio divestitures. Sutton repeatedly pointed to the company's adaptability and indicated, "we have really urgent work to do."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from cautious optimism and a focus on overcoming headwinds to urgency and an execution-centric message. Analysts shifted from immediate operational concerns toward probing the credibility and sustainability of the new leadership's plans and pricing achievements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The CEO transition from De Lyle Bloomquist to Scott Sutton marked a change in leadership emphasis, with Sutton prioritizing urgent execution, free cash flow, and a holistic value maximization strategy versus Bloomquist's focus on cost reductions, asset divestitures, and high-return project development.
  • Guidance language shifted from a specific EBITDA range and project milestones to an execution-driven plan to "deliver positive free cash flow in 2026" and improve EBITDA across all businesses. The new CEO downplayed asset divestiture, favoring improvement and retention.
  • Strategic focus evolved from targeted cost cuts and asset sales to dynamic portfolio management and new product commercialization across all segments.
  • Analysts' tone remains skeptical but interested, with more focus on the credibility of management’s new approach and the ability to deliver on ambitious pricing and margin targets.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted the unsustainability of negative free cash flow and high-cost debt, making positive free cash flow in 2026 a mission-critical target.
  • Pricing sustainability in Cellulose Specialties remains a risk, with the CEO noting, "we've got a long way to go before we get to reinvestment economics."
  • Competitive pressure from subsidized imports, especially from China in ethers and from Brazil and Norway in specialty cellulose, is a significant challenge. Trade remedy investigations are ongoing, with preliminary determinations expected in the coming months.
  • Operational challenges at Tartas are being addressed by a crisis management team, with some early success but continued risk.
  • The remaining 15% of Cellulose Specialties business is not yet secured and may result in further volume loss or require higher pricing to close.

Final Takeaway

Rayonier Advanced Materials management is focused on delivering positive free cash flow in 2026, asserting leadership in Cellulose Specialties through substantial price increases and targeted volume management, and driving EBITDA improvements across all business segments through execution of leadership initiatives, dynamic asset allocation, and new product commercialization. The company is addressing high-cost debt and operational challenges while navigating external risks from trade and competition, with the intent to exit 2026 with significant momentum and a streamlined, value-maximizing portfolio.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Most and least shorted energy stocks with over $2B market cap as of Feb
data.description:
In February, the S&P 500 energy sector (XLE) moved nearly 12% in the upward direction this year supported by a 8.3% rise in crude oil futures (CL1:COM) over potential disruption in global crude supply.

Against this backdrop, short interest trends among mid, large and mega-cap energy stocks show a wide divergence. Solaris Energy Infrastructure (SEI) was the most shorted stock in February, while MPLX (MPLX) was the least shorted stock.

Most shorted energy stocks as of February end:

  • Solaris Energy Infrastructure (SEI) 24.05%
  • Centrus Energy (LEU) 23.63%
  • Northern Oil and Gas (NOG) 19.17%
  • Delek US Holdings (DK) 18.12%
  • PBF Energy (PBF) 16.13%
 

Least shorted energy stocks as of February end:

  • MPLX (MPLX) 0.74%
  • Energy Transfer (ET) 0.85%
  • Alliance Resource Partners (ARLP) 0.95%
  • Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) 1.00%
  • Mach Natural Resources (MNR) 1.02%

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), and (IXC).

data.title: U.S. stocks inch up as Wall Street assesses the latest labor report; oil prices ease
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages crawled higher on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 ((SP500)) and the Nasdaq ((COMP:IND)) leading the advance as oil prices fell for the first time since the U.S.-Israel-Iran war escalations and chip stocks recovered. 

Traders also digested a better-than-expected labor report and a lower U.S. Composite PMI read.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.9%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.4%, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.6%.

U.S. private sector employment grew by 63K jobs in February, topping the +43K consensus and accelerating from the 11K added in January, according to data released by ADP on Wednesday.

In addition, the February S&P Global U.S. Composite PMI came in lower than expected at 51.9 from 53.0 in January. 

Moreover, U.S. economic activity rose at a "slight to moderate" pace, according to the Federal Reserve's Beige Book report. Lastly, mortgage refinance applications increased for the fourth straight week to the strongest pace since 2022.

“My main pushback on the AI doomsaying for the labor market is that AI is not in charge; we are. Leadership matters. Also, policy (at various levels) can help encourage pro-worker AI outcomes other than automation. The private market on its own might not do enough,” said Claudia Sahm, chief economist for New Century Advisors.

On commodities, oil prices (CL1:COM) initially fell on Wednesday for the first time since the start of the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict before eventually rising again.

U.S. Treasury yields were slightly higher across the curve.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 2 basis points to 4.09%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) advanced 4 basis points to 3.55%.

The Trump Administration is expected to implement the 15% global tariff rate sometime this week, according to Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent.

data.title: Daktronics signals strong Q4 and FY27 revenue momentum with $342M backlog and robust live events pipeline
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Daktronics (DAKT) Q3 2026

Management View

  • Bradley Wiemann, Executive Vice President & Advisor to the CEO, highlighted "revenue growth of 21.6% year-over-year despite the effectively shorter work period due to the 3 major holidays as well as adverse weather conditions throughout the quarter," noting efficient manufacturing and major project completions, including five Major League Baseball stadiums and the University of Illinois Football's video scoring system.
  • Wiemann reported, "orders in the quarter were once again over $200 million," with new order growth at 7.6%, elevating the product and services backlog to $342 million, "25% higher than it was this time last year."
  • The acquisition of intellectual property and engineering teams from X Display Company (XDC) was announced, expanding micro-LED and micro IC capabilities and enhancing high-resolution product offerings.
  • Wiemann emphasized record order growth in the Transportation segment, particularly airport and intelligent transportation system projects, and highlighted the launch of innovative products, including next-generation indoor video solutions and digital audio facades.
  • Howard Atkins, Acting CFO, stated, "As orders have grown, so has revenue, which at $182 million in the quarter grew more than 20% year-over-year, mostly due to efficient order conversion by our manufacturing teams during the quarter."
  • Atkins further noted, "Gross profit margin in the quarter was essentially flat to the year ago quarter at 24%... offset by the fact that the backlog fulfillment in the quarter was largely from the lower-margin live events business line."
  • Atkins reported, "Daktronics third quarter '26 net income after tax was $3 million or $0.06 per fully diluted share," with adjusted net income of $4.6 million after accounting for nonrecurring expenses.
  • Ramesh Jayaraman, CEO and President, marked the completion of a CEO transition, stating that his primary focus is a "look-listen-learn tour" to refine strategies for growth and operational excellence, and invited investors to an upcoming Investor Day for further strategic updates.

Outlook

  • Wiemann stated, "Our product and services backlog grew to $342 million coming into Q4 and is 25% higher than it was this time last year."
  • Jayaraman described entering the final quarter with "very strong momentum, strong end market demand and a strong backlog tailwind," targeting "a strong finish of fiscal '26 through efficient revenue conversion and expense and productivity management."
  • Atkins provided that "the combination of a high backlog...with what we're seeing as a good pipeline already in the fourth quarter, good order momentum sets us up well for good top and bottom-line finish to the year."

Financial Results

  • Atkins reported revenue of $182 million for the quarter, with gross profit margin at 24% and net income after tax of $3 million, or $0.06 per fully diluted share.
  • Adjusted net income was $4.6 million after accounting for $1.6 million in nonrecurring management transition and acquisition expenses.
  • Product backlog at quarter-end was $342 million, up 25% from the prior year.
  • Atkins noted a cash balance of $144 million, a 13% increase from the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025, and share repurchases totaling approximately 1.3 million shares during the first nine months.
  • The company converted its commercial bank backup credit line to a cash-flow facility and reported no borrowings under the line of credit.

Q&A

  • Aaron Spychalla, Craig-Hallum Capital Group: Questioned pipeline strength and win rates in live events. Wiemann responded that the pipeline is "robust, strong going into this next year" and highlighted "6 of 6" wins in Major League Baseball projects. Jayaraman added the company is balancing growth with a "balanced portfolio" approach across segments.
  • Spychalla followed up on commercial market and channel expansion. Wiemann confirmed "on-premise as well as our both Spectaculars and out-of-home market...are still looking promising," and noted AV integrator growth as "a big part of our overall growth strategy."
  • On operational initiatives and margin trajectory, Atkins stated, "We're well into the game" on initiatives and "more than 1/3 to 1/2 into the actual realization of the benefit," while acknowledging ongoing geopolitical risks and adaptability.
  • Anja Soderstrom, Sidoti: Asked about backlog mix impact on margins. Atkins pointed to the revenue mix table and reiterated gross margin sensitivity to fixed costs and seasonality.
  • Soderstrom inquired about the Mexico facility. Wiemann confirmed it is "on track" to be operational in Q1 FY27, with minimal impact on gross margins as the facility will be leased.
  • Soderstrom asked about a key commercial account delay. Wiemann explained it was due to an acquisition by the customer and expected order flow to resume in Q4.
  • Soderstrom asked about customer sentiment and M&A appetite. Wiemann said "projects are moving forward...we're not foreseeing any reduction" in US market activity. Atkins and Jayaraman described active consideration of tuck-in M&A, emphasizing financial discipline.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst questions were probing but generally positive, focusing on pipeline, margin trajectory, and execution risks rather than expressing skepticism.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in prepared remarks, with Wiemann and Jayaraman expressing strong momentum and Atkins highlighting resilient financials. In the Q&A, management was detailed and responsive, with some caution regarding macro uncertainties and margin headwinds.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was similarly upbeat but added emphasis on backlog-driven momentum and operational progress, while analysts maintained a constructive tone.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter's guidance language shifted to highlight a "very strong momentum" and a "multi-quarter runway on our revenue" compared to the previous quarter's focus on transformation and margin expansion.
  • Strategic focus broadened further with the XDC acquisition and ongoing product innovation.
  • Analysts remained concentrated on backlog conversion, margin sustainability, and channel expansion, similar to the prior quarter.
  • Financial results in Q3 included a lower net income after tax ($3 million) compared to Q2 ($17.5 million), reflecting seasonal impacts and higher tariff costs.
  • Management's confidence in operational initiatives and backlog-driven growth remained consistent, but with added detail on segment performance and new product launches.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty, with Atkins stating, "There is risk out there and a lot of uncertainty...as the world changes, we'll adapt to the world."
  • Tariff expenses were highlighted as a margin headwind, with an "extra $6 million" in the current quarter.
  • Backlog fulfillment remains heavily weighted to lower-margin live events, which could continue to pressure gross margins.
  • Analysts raised questions about margin impact from backlog mix, customer account delays, and the pace of operational benefit realization.

Final Takeaway

Management described the third quarter as a period of robust revenue and order growth, driven by successful execution in live events and transportation, continued innovation, and a strengthened cash position. Heading into the final quarter, Daktronics is focused on efficient order conversion, disciplined expense management, and leveraging a record-high backlog to support multi-quarter revenue growth. The leadership transition was described as smooth, with strategic updates expected at the upcoming Investor Day. The company remains vigilant regarding macro risks, while maintaining a positive outlook for continued profitable growth into fiscal 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Mondelez may go small and highly targeted with its M&A strategy
data.description:
Mondelez International (MDLZ) plans to take a selective approach to M&A this year. Management has described M&A as a selective, bolt‑on tool to support its snacking strategy, although valuations are seen as limiting the number of viable targets.

The Food Dive website recently highlighted that over the last few years, companies have been looking for acquisitions as a way to add higher-growth, trendier brands to the mix to rejuvenate slowing sales. However, that has also created a more competitive market for potential buyers.

At the 2026 CAGNY conference, Mondelez (MDLZ) CEO Dirk Van de Put and CFO Luca Zaramella reiterated that targeted growth‑accretive acquisitions remain part of Mondelez's (MDLZ) overall capital allocation framework alongside brand reinvestment, dividends, and buybacks. They both stressed maintaining a strong balance sheet while pursuing strategic M&A to strengthen core biscuits, chocolate, and baked snacks positions, particularly in under‑indexed geographies and adjacencies like cakes, pastries, and premium chocolate.

Notably, Mondelez (MDLZ) still runs a structured M&A sourcing process, assembling an annual wish list of roughly 40 targets, largely within its core snacking spaces. Priority areas include expanding its cakes and pastries platform and premium chocolate portfolio, where management believes scale, distribution, and marketing can unlock outsized synergies. 

Since 2018, Mondelez (MDLZ) has executed about ten snacking transactions, including deals for Clif Bar & Company, premium cookie brand Tate’s Bake Shop, refrigerated bar maker Perfect Snacks, premium chocolate and snack brand Hu, and regional players like Chipita and Ricolino.

data.title: Big Tech trade group warns Anthropic ban could hinder tech access
data.description:
A Big Tech trade group has expressed concern over the Trump administration declaring Anthropic (ANTRHO) a "supply chain risk" because it won't allow its AI technology to be used by the U.S. military for domestic mass surveillance or fully autonomous weaponry. 

"We are concerned ​by recent reports regarding the Department of War’s ⁠consideration of imposing a supply chain risk designation in ​response to a procurement dispute," the Information Technology Industry Council said in a letter Wednesday to War Secretary Pete Hegseth, according to Reuters. The news agency noted that Anthropic wasn't specifically named in the letter.

The letter went on to warn that the supply chain risk designation could "undermine the government’s access to the best-in-class products and services from American companies ​that serve all agencies and components of the ​federal ⁠government."

Several leading tech companies belong to the trade group, including Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Alphabet's Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), IBM (IBM), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Intel (INTC), Meta (META), and Broadcom (AVGO), according to the group's website.

The Trump administration designated Anthropic (ANTHRO) a "supply chain risk" last week, which means that government agencies are no longer allowed to use its AI technology and government contractors are barred from doing business with the company.

data.title: BTIG’s Jonathan Krinsky: Investors should shift to an offensive positioning strategy
data.description:
BTIG’s Jonathan Krinsky is signaling that investors should shift from a defensive to an offensive positioning strategy as the S&P 500 (SP500) rebounds from recent lows.

“A low has been made, and we should be playing offense more than defense,” the chief market technician wrote in a note to clients as the equity benchmark climbed above 6,800, rising as much as 1% on Wednesday.

The recovery comes after the S&P 500 (SP500) experienced a 2.5% intraday drop on Tuesday, with better-than-expected labor market data and an expansion in services activity helping to stabilize the index.

According to Krinsky, the gauge has now climbed above a key technical level that makes it likely bearish bettors will get caught unawares when stocks bounce back on any new dips. The upbeat economic data helped offset worries around the continuing conflict between Iran and the U.S. in the Middle East.

In the strategist’s view, several sectors appear to have found a floor as the S&P 500 (SP500) reclaims key support levels.

“We see bottoms in airlines (JETS), consumer (XLP), (XLY), banks (KBE), (KRE), (KBWB), crypto (BTC-USD), (ETH-USD), software (IGPT), (XSW), (IGV), and China (MCHI), (GXC), (FXI), while energy (XLE) and staples (XLP) look like tactical tops,” Krinsky wrote.

Energy stocks (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE) have widened their 2026 rally to 24% amid concerns over the conflict, while industrial stocks (XLI), (VIS), (FXR), (IYJ) have fallen as much as 2.5% across three sessions due to rising oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM).

Despite the recent bullish signals, the S&P 500 (SP500) has had a listless performance since October, according to the technician’s analysis.

While the index climbed above 7,000 points for the first time in late January, it had trimmed those gains by the end of the session, and traders continue watching to see if the gauge can close above that milestone.

Krinsky’s bullish outlook aligns with recent positive calls from other major Wall Street institutions. Goldman Sachs strategists led by Peter Oppenheimer said investors should view any correction in equities as “a buying opportunity rather than the beginning of a bear market,” while Citadel Securities’ Scott Rubner indicated his fundamental analysis signaled that now was the time to turn bullish on equities.
